concept: Pix2Pix requires paired training examples (exact sketch-photo match), which is expensive or impossible to gather for many tasks. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: CycleGAN.'

exercises.explain: Before learning Pix2Pix limitations, remember that Pix2Pix translates images (like sketches) directly into realistic outputs!
exercises.prompt: What is the main purpose of a Pix2Pix network?

options.text: It translates one image style directly into another

cards.body: Imagine trying to train an AI to turn sketches into photos, but you must find a real photo for every single doodle you draw. Sounds exhausting, right? That's the paired data problem!
cards.title: The Perfect Match Hunt

cards.body: Paired training data consists of dataset examples where every input image has an exact, corresponding target image (like a sketch of a shoe and the exact real shoe in the same position).
cards.title: What is Paired Data?

cards.body: Think of paired data like a bilingual dictionary where every word has a direct, one-to-one translation. Unpaired data is like having a book in English and a book in French, but they tell completely different stories!
cards.title: The Dictionary Analogy

cards.body: To train a Pix2Pix model to convert daytime street scenes to nighttime scenes, you need photos taken from the exact same camera angle at 12:00 PM and 12:00 AM. If a car moves, the pair is broken!
cards.title: The Moving Car Problem

cards.body: You might think we can easily generate paired data for everything. But how do you get a paired photo of a real dinosaur next to a sketch? Or a painting by Monet of a modern smartphone? Some pairs are physically impossible to create.
cards.title: Myth: We Can Always Get Pairs
Paired vs. Unpaired
cards.body: Let's look at the difference between what Pix2Pix needs versus what we often actually have in the real world.

right.text: Random cat sketches and random cat photos that don't match up.

left.text: Exact match: Sketch of a cat next to a photo of the same cat in the same pose.

exercises.explain: Pix2Pix needs a direct, one-to-one mapping between the source image and the target image in the dataset.
exercises.prompt: Why is gathering data for Pix2Pix so difficult?

options.text: It requires exact, aligned pairs of input and output images.
It works best with completely random, unrelated images.
It does not need any target images to learn.

exercises.prompt: True or False: It is always easy and cheap to find paired training data for any image translation task.
exercises.explain: For many real-world tasks (like turning horses into zebras), we cannot get exact matching photos of the same animal in the same pose.

exercises.sentence: Pix2Pix cannot train properly without [blank] datasets.
exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank: Pix2Pix cannot train properly without _____ datasets.
exercises.explain: Pix2Pix depends on paired data, where each input has a corresponding, identical-layout target image.
Having photos of the same street in both day and night from the exact same camera angle represents paired data. Monet paintings of arbitrary landscapes and random photos do not match up directly.

options.text: A collection of random photos of horses and random photos of zebras.
A photo of a building at noon and a photo of the same building at midnight from the same spot.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is an example of paired training data?
If we want to translate horses to zebras but don't have matching photos, what should we do?
exercises.explain: Because paired data is so hard to find, researchers developed new methods that don't need exact pairs. Next up: CycleGAN.

options.text: Find a way to manually draw millions of perfect matching photos.
Use a new architecture like CycleGAN that can learn from unpaired data.
Give up on image translation entirely.

title: The Need for Paired Training Data
